items.q: What is the difference between nominal and real values?
items.correct: Nominal values are measured in current money; real values are adjusted for inflation to allow meaningful comparison over time.
items.misconception: Nominal means estimated and real means accurate.
items.random_wrong: Nominal values are written and real values are spent.
items.why: Both nominal and real values are 'accurate' — they answer different questions. Real values strip out inflation to reveal actual purchasing power changes. Critical for comparing wages or GDP across years.

items.q: What did the original Phillips curve suggest, and how has thinking changed?
items.correct: The original Phillips curve suggested a stable inverse relationship between unemployment and inflation. Friedman and the 1970s stagflation experience added expectations — the long-run Phillips curve is vertical at the natural rate of unemployment.
items.misconception: The Phillips curve still proves you can always reduce unemployment by allowing more inflation.
items.random_wrong: The Phillips curve shows the link between phone use and unemployment.
items.why: The simple inverse trade-off broke down empirically. Modern theory distinguishes short-run (trade-off possible) from long-run (no trade-off). Students learn the original curve as if still valid.

items.correct: Unemployment caused by a mismatch between workers' skills/location and available jobs — often due to long-term economic change like deindustrialisation.
items.misconception: Structural unemployment is unemployment caused by a recession.
items.random_wrong: Structural unemployment is when people are too lazy to work.
items.why: Recession-driven unemployment is CYCLICAL. Structural is about long-term mismatches — coal miners can't easily become app developers. Students collapse all unemployment into 'lack of demand'.

items.q: Which is NOT a factor of production?
items.why: Correct answer: Money. “Land” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The answer examiners want is: The whole economy. “The price of bread” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many factors of production are there?
items.why: Exact answer: 4 factors. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
